body:
Regulation: B.C. Reg. 38/2026
Made under: Intimate Images Protection Statutes Amendment Act
Published in: No. 5 – B.C. Regs. 29 to 42, March 24, 2026
Deposit date: March 18, 2026

B.C. Reg. 38/2026, deposited March 18, 2026, under theINTIMATE IMAGES PROTECTION STATUTES AMENDMENT ACT, 2025[section 13] andCIVIL RESOLUTION TRIBUNAL ACT[section 93]. Order in Council 96/2026, approved and ordered March 18, 2026. On the recommendation of the undersigned, the Lieutenant Governor, by and with the advice and consent of the Executive Council, orders that (a)	sections 1 to 9 of theIntimate Images Protection Statutes Amendment Act, 2025, S.B.C. 2025, c. 21, are brought into force, and (b)	the attached Intimate Image Claims Regulation is made. — N. SHARMA,Attorney General and Deputy Premier; A. DIX,Presiding Member of the Executive Council. INTIMATE IMAGE CLAIMS REGULATION 1In this regulation,"Act"means theCivil Resolution Tribunal Act. 2This regulation applies to intimate image claims under Division 8[Intimate Image Claims]of Part 10[Tribunal Jurisdiction]of the Act. Maximum intimate image claim amount 3For the purposes of the definition of "tribunal limit amount" in section 136.1[definitions for Division – intimate image claims]of the Act, $75 000 is the maximum amount that the tribunal may award in an intimate image claim in accordance with section 136.2 (1) (b)[claims within jurisdiction of tribunal for intimate image claims]. Index Page|Previous Regulation|Next Regulation|Cumulative Index Page Copyright © 2026: King's Printer, Victoria, British Columbia, Canada

minutes_briefs.title: BC sets $75,000 limit for intimate image claims at Civil Resolution Tribunal
minutes_briefs.summary: British Columbia has set a maximum of $75,000 that the Civil Resolution Tribunal (a public body that resolves disputes without going to court) can award in claims involving intimate images shared without consent. This regulation brings parts of the Intimate Images Protection Statutes Amendment Act into force, creating a new process for victims to seek compensation through the tribunal instead of the courts.
